title: Comparative Sensory Processing
description: A graduate course on how sensory systems solve their computational problems — and on what the comparison between modalities reveals about olfaction.
intro: Most sensory neuroscience is taught one modality at a time, as a tour of anatomy: receptors, then relays, then cortex. This course is organised the other way round. Each module begins with a computational problem — what must the animal infer, from what, under what loss, on what deadline — and treats the biology as evidence about how that problem is solved.

The reason for doing it this way is comparative. Vision and audition have mature normative theories: we can derive retinal receptive fields and cochlear filters from the statistics of natural input and check the answer against the biology. Olfaction has no theory of comparable maturity. The wager of this course is that this gap is informative, and that the clearest route to a theory of smell runs through the modalities that already have one.

So every module uses the same eleven-section skeleton, in the same order, so that §4 always means the receptor array whether you are reading about cones or odorant receptors. You should be able to read across modules as easily as down one. Where a structure is absent — and olfaction is missing several that vision teaches you to expect — the section says so and asks why.
samplerNote: Three modalities in full — vision, audition, olfaction — using an identical eleven-section skeleton so they can be read across as easily as down. The full course adds somatosensation, gustation, vestibular, proprioception and the exotic senses.

modules.label: Module 0
modules.title: The Normative Toolkit
modules.blurb: Why the comparison works, the lessons it yields, and the shared vocabulary the modality modules assume.

subsections.title: Why compare modalities
subsections.blurb: Sensory systems as natural experiments, and the seven lessons the course is built around.

subsections.title: Three normative stances
subsections.blurb: Efficient coding, Bayesian inference, and task optimality make different predictions. Telling them apart is the core skill.

subsections.title: Efficient coding in two regimes
subsections.blurb: Whiten at high SNR, pool at low SNR — the cleanest quantitative prediction in sensory neuroscience.

modules.label: Module 1
modules.title: Vision
modules.blurb: The reference modality. Taught first and deepest, because it supplies the templates everything else is measured against.

subsections.title: The computational problem
subsections.blurb: What must be inferred, from what, under what loss, on what deadline.

subsections.blurb: The sensor as a physical device: noise floor, bandwidth, dynamic range.

subsections.title: The receptor array
subsections.blurb: Channel count, tuning breadth, ordered or unordered.

subsections.title: The first circuit — retina
subsections.blurb: Centre–surround as whitening, and the confirmed prediction that made the theory credible.

subsections.title: Routing — the thalamus and what it is for
subsections.blurb: The LGN barely re-codes its input. Decomposing what it does is the preparation for asking how olfaction manages without one.

subsections.title: The central representation — V1
subsections.blurb: Sparse coding derives V1-like receptive fields — the deepest any normative theory has reached, which is not very deep.

subsections.title: Parallel streams
subsections.blurb: Identity, location, value, action.

subsections.blurb: How movement shapes the input; sampling as experiment design.

subsections.title: Adaptation, gain control, and state
subsections.blurb: Across timescales, milliseconds to lifetime.

subsections.title: Normative synthesis and the olfaction hook
subsections.blurb: Where efficient coding came closest to proof, and where its limits are clearest.

modules.label: Module 2
modules.title: Audition
modules.blurb: The mixture problem in its purest form, and the second modality whose receptor array was derived from natural statistics.

subsections.title: The first circuit — cochlear nucleus
subsections.blurb: Not compression, not convergence: parallel decomposition into streams that preserve different things.

subsections.title: Routing — the obligatory hub
subsections.blurb: More subcortical stations than any other sense, and one that almost nothing bypasses.

subsections.title: The central representation — A1
subsections.blurb: Tonotopic, and then it gets hard. A1 has resisted a normative account, which is itself a finding.

subsections.title: Normative synthesis and the olfaction hook
subsections.blurb: The second great derivation of a receptor array — and the same failure at depth that vision shows.

modules.label: Module 3
modules.title: Olfaction
modules.blurb: The same eleven sections, asked of a modality where almost every assumption vision teaches you turns out to fail.

subsections.blurb: Three-layer paleocortex where recurrence dominates afferent drive. An associative memory, not a sensory map.

modules.blurb: What these four comparisons alone license: candidate analogues, the disanalogies that break them, and testable predictions.
